coredata.dc:title: Bacterial responsive hydrogels based on quaternized chitosan and GQDs-ε-PL for chemo-photothermal synergistic anti-infection in diabetic wounds

Zhong, Haowei
Zhang, Yuan
Gao, Xiaoran
Wang, Jianmin
Liu, Jingfeng
Han, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Clinically, systemic antibiotic therapy and traditional dressings care are not satisfactory in treating chronic diabetic ulcers (DU). Therefore, we presented sprayable antibacterial hydrogel for effective treatment of DU by using antibacterial macromolecules (quaternized chitosan, QCS, Mn ≈ 1.5 × 105), photothermal antibacterial nanoparticles (ε-poly-l-lysine grafted graphene quantum dots, GQDs-ε-PL) and miocompatible macromolecules (benzaldehyde-terminated four-arm poly(ethylene glycol), 4 arm PEG-BA) as materials. The results revealed that the hydrogel could be in situ formed in 70–89 s through dynamic imine bonds crosslinking and exhibited a pH-dependent swelling ability and degradability. The hydrogel could respond to bacterial triggered acidic environment to play a synergistic effect of chemotherapy and xenon light irradiated PTT, leading to the rupture of the bacterial membrane and the inactivation of bacteria, promoting the migration and proliferation of fibroblast cell, enhancing the adhesion of platelet endothelial cell, and finally accelerating the healing of infected diabetic wound. Moreover, the hydrogel displayed self-healing, hemostatic, and biocompatible abilities, which could provide a better healing environment for wound and further promote wound healing. Hence, the multifunctional hydrogel is expected to be a potential dressing for the clinical treatment of DU.

The data are shown as the mean ± SD (n = 6). **P < 0.01, *P < 0.05.

head.ce:title: Bacterial responsive hydrogels based on quaternized chitosan and GQDs-ε-PL for chemo-photothermal synergistic anti-infection in diabetic wounds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clinically, systemic antibiotic therapy and traditional dressings care are not satisfactory in treating chronic diabetic ulcers (DU). Therefore, we presented sprayable antibacterial hydrogel for effective treatment of DU by using antibacterial macromolecules (quaternized chitosan, QCS, Mn ≈ 1.5 × 105), photothermal antibacterial nanoparticles (ε-poly-l-lysine grafted graphene quantum dots, GQDs-ε-PL) and miocompatible macromolecules (benzaldehyde-terminated four-arm poly(ethylene glycol), 4 arm PEG-BA) as materials. The results revealed that the hydrogel could be in situ formed in 70–89 s through dynamic imine bonds crosslinking and exhibited a pH-dependent swelling ability and degradability. The hydrogel could respond to bacterial triggered acidic environment to play a synergistic effect of chemotherapy and xenon light irradiated PTT, leading to the rupture of the bacterial membrane and the inactivation of bacteria, promoting the migration and proliferation of fibroblast cell, enhancing the adhesion of platelet endothelial cell, and finally accelerating the healing of infected diabetic wound. Moreover, the hydrogel displayed self-healing, hemostatic, and biocompatible abilities, which could provide a better healing environment for wound and further promote wound healing. Hence, the multifunctional hydrogel is expected to be a potential dressing for the clinical treatment of DU.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetic ulcer (DU) is one of the main chronic complications of diabetes [1]. Its pathogenesis is complicated [2], mainly including neuropathy, vascular lesions and infection [3]. In particular, the bacterial colonization and microbial infections can trigger continuous inflammatory responses and make the wounds worse and unable to heal. In severe cases, it may even lead to amputation and death [4,5]. Hence, the anti-infective treatment for DU is crucial. Clinically, systemic antibiotic therapy is often used to treat DU infections [6]. However, the treatment effect is not very satisfactory, and it may also cause drug resistance [7]. Recently, local anti-infective adjuvant treatments such as local debridement [8], topical antibiotic application [9], and antibacterial dressings [10–12] have certain antibacterial effects, but these methods are still difficult to meet clinical needs. It is necessary to develop an effective strategy to intelligently target and address the above problems. Recently studies have found that hydrogels [13] have the advantages of antibacterial, breathable, moisture permeability, control and absorption of exudates, avoid wound adhesion, etc., which can effectively promote the healing of DU wounds [14–17]. Especially the recently emerging photothermal antibacterial hydrogels, which can generate heat by irradiating photosensitizers with light, resulting in the destruction of bacterial cell membranes, the denaturation of proteins, and the dispersion of biofilm, has exhibited great potentials in effective antibacterial photothermal therapy (PTT) [18–20]. Currently, many photosensitizers, such as aggregation-induced emission [21], gold nanoparticles [22], carbon dots (CDs) [23], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [24], black phosphorus [25], organic dye [26], etc., have been studied their potential use in PTT. However, black phosphorus and organic dye are easily degraded and the photothermal persistence depends on their concentration, and gold nanoparticles are expensive and difficult to prepare. In comparison, GQDs are cheap, stable, low toxic, photoluminescent and biocompatible, which can produce photothermal effects under visible (i.e. xenon light) or near-infrared light [27,28]. In addition, the surface of GQDs is rich in functional group and easy to modify. After surface modification, GQDs are more easily loaded by hydrogels to form photothermal antibacterial hydrogels. However, because excessive local temperature is easy to burn the tissues and cause secondary damage to the wound, it is not enough to treat DU only with PTT. Therefore, some researchers utilized the synergistic effect of chemotherapy and PTT as a promising strategy for resistant bacterial infection and DU therapy [29,30]. For example, Tong et al. [31] constructed PB@PDA@Ag nanocomplexes, which could generate photothermal effect and released Ag+ under near infrared laser irradiation to radicate multidrug-resistant bacteria and accelerate wound healing of MRSA-infected diabetic wound. Li et al. [32] propose a photothermal-activated multifunctional nano-antibacterial platform (MoS2/ICG/Ag) that has the combined chemo/photothermal/photodynamic triple-mode antibacterial capability under a single 808 nm laser irradiation. It has been successfully used for treating S. aureus biofilms-infected wound in vivo with minimal toxicity. It can be seen from these reports that chemo/photothermal combination treatment for resistant bacterial infection and DU therapy is effective, but nano-formulations are generally used in the reports, the hydrogels with similar ideal dressing properties are rarely used.

Consequently, we designed an in situ sprayable, bacteria and xenon light (XL)-responsive, self-healable, antibacterial hydrogel for chemo/photothermal treatment with DU. The functionalized hydrogel includes (1) Quaternized chitosan (QCS) and benzaldehyde-terminated four-arm poly(ethylene glycol) (4 arm PEG-BA) based hydrogel skeleton materials, which can form into hydrogel quickly by in situ gelation and spray gelation. No matter what shapes or areas of the wounds, the above solutions of QCS and 4 arm PEG-BA can be sprayed at the wounds site to form hydrogels in situ for wound-healing. Simultaneously, since QCS is modified natural macromolecules and 4 arm PEG-BA is biocompatible [33,34], the biocompatibility of the hydrogels is also predictable; (2) dynamic covalent bond crosslinking points, which are unstable and easily undergone protonation [35,36] under bacterial triggered acidic wound environment [37,38], and then result in the degradation of the hydrogel to release more antibacterial substances (QCS and GQDs-ε-PL) to play an “intelligent” therapeutic role on DU. Moreover, the dynamic imine bond have dynamic characteristics, once broken, the dynamic covalent bonds can re-crosslink, and recover the hydrogel by itself to continue seal the wound and prolong the life span of the dressings; (3) chemo-photothermal agent: ε-poly-l-lysine grafted GQDs (GQDs-ε-PL), whose surface is rich in amino groups, so that GQDs-ε-PL could be uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel network through dynamic imine bond crosslinking and endow the xenon light (wavelength range: 400–1100 nm) responsive photothermal performance and chemo-photothermal synergistic bactericidal effect to the hydrogel, which will reduce inflammation via chemical sterilization and photothermal therapy (PTT). Additionally, the introduction of GQDs-ε-PL also further improves the mechanical properties of the hydrogel, and makes the hydrogel more likely to be used as a dressing.

Based on the above ideas, GQDs-ε-PL/QCS/4 arm PEG-BA hydrogels were prepared and studied in this work. The gelation time, morphological structure, rheological properties, injectability, self-healing ability, photothermal performance, swelling ability and degradability, cytocompatibility, hemostatic performance, in vitro antibacterial properties and in vivo wound healing effect on the infected diabetic wounds of the hydrogels were investigated, which is expected to provide an “intelligent” strategy for anti-infective and multifunctional treatment of DU.

Graphene, 1-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium nitrate and ammonia (NH3∙H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Quaternized chitosan (QCS, 50% degree of substitution, Mn ≈ 1.5 × 105) was purchased from Lvshen Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Qidong, China); ε-poly-l-lysine (ε-PL, Mn ≈ 3500) and cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from Meilun Biotech Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China). Quaternized chitosan was purchased from Lvshen Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Qidong, China); Benzaldehyde-terminated four-arm poly(ethylene glycol) (4 arm PEG-BA) was purchased from Ziqi Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) were obtained from Lezhen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared based on modified hummers method [39]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ε-poly-l-lysine grafted GQDs (GQDs-ε-PL) was synthesized by using the previous method with slight modification [27]. In brief, 100 mL of GO solution (1 mg/mL), 10 mL of NH3∙H2O and 5% H2O2 were mixed and reacted in Teflon-lined autoclave reactor at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature (RT), the reaction solution was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane filter, concentrated, purified by dialysis method (MWCO 500) for 2 d and lyophilized to obtain dry GQDs. Then, -COOH of GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) was activated by adding EDC and NHS, and certain amount of ε-PL (the molar ratio of -COOH on GQDs and -NH2 on ε-PL was 1:60) was added into the above solution with stirring for 24 h. Thus, the dry GQDs-ε-PL was obtained by further dialyzed (MWCO 3000) and lyophilized. The grafting ratio was 82.69%, and the amino content of GQDs-ε-PL was 6.45 mmol/g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-ε-PL/QCS/4 arm PEG-BA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: First, GQDs-ε-PL, QCS, and 4ARM-PEG-CHO were dissolved in deionized water at a concentration of 0.2 g/mL, 0.03 g/mL, and 0.15 g/mL, respectively. Second, the above solutions with certain amounts were mixed evenly in a centrifuge tube to prepare GQDs-ε-PL/QCS/4 arm PEG-BA hydrogel. By changing the feeding ratio of GQDs-ε-PL, QCS, and 4arm-PEG-BA, the molar ratio of -NH2 on GQDs-ε-PL and QCS and -CHO on 4 arm-PEG-BA was changed, and then a series of hydrogels were obtained (seeing Table 1 ), which were named as Gel0-Gel5.

The fluorescence spectra of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL was measured by spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse); The UV–vis spectra of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL was recorded by microplate reader (Infinite M200 PRO, Tecan, Austria); The micromorphology of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi-H600); The chemical structure of GQDs, GQDs-ε-PL, Gel1 and the solid mixture of GQDs-ε-PL, QCS and 4arm-PEG-BA was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet 360) in the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. The network structure of Gel0-Gel5 was observed by Tungsten filament scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250); To visualize Gel0-Gel5 in fluorescence imaging form, Gel0-Gel5 were frozen and cut into 10 μm slices by cryostat microtome (HM525 NX), respectively. The sections were placed on the confocal glass slides and air dried. The fluorescence photographs were captured by excitation of GQDs-ε-PL in the hydrogel at 488 nm by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM 510, Zeiss, Germany).

The gelation time of Gel0-Gel5 at different temperature was determined by tube inverting method [40,41]. To verify whether the hydrogel can be formed by spraying, the mixture solution of GQDs-ε-PL and QCS in one spray bottle and 4 arm-PEG-BA solution in another spray bottle was sprayed onto the surface of fresh pigskin at the same time at RT. The sol-gel phase transition process was observed under the visible (VIS) and UV254 nm light. To examine the deformability of the hydrogel, Gel1 was added into “bow knot, pentagram and heart” molds, and observed under VIS and UV254 nm light. To examine the injectablity after gelation, Gel1 was in situ formed in 1 mL of syringe completely, and then Gel1 was pushed from the syringe into the watch glass. The injectable process was taken photos under VIS and UV254 nm light.

GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL (4 mg/mL) were put in 2 mL of centrifuge tube and irradiated by xenon light (XL, wavelength range: 400–1100 nm, 1 W/cm2) for 0–10 min. The infrared thermal imaging was recorded by thermal imager (Fluke Ti25).

Gel0-Gel5 were put in 2 mL of centrifuge tube and irradiated by XL (1 W/cm2) for 0–15 min. The infrared thermal imaging was recorded by thermal imager. And then the temperature change curves were drawn by recorded temperature in the intrinsic time intervals. To investigate the thermo-stability of the hydrogels, Gel1 was chosen as representative to go through four times XL irradiation on/off cycles to draw temperature change curves.

The gelation behavior of Gel0-Gel5 was measured by rheometer (MCR302, Anton Paar). Each sample was transferred directly to the bottom plate of the rheometer, the outer edges of the samples were sealed with silicone oil and held between two plates to prevent evaporation of water during rheological tests. First, frequency sweeps of Gel0-Gel5 and XL treated Gel1 were collected over a wide frequency range (strain (γ) = 1.0%, angular frequency (ω) = 1–10 rad s−1) with a frequency of 1 Hz. Second, temperature sweeps of Gel0-Gel5 with a frequency of 1 Hz were conducted over the temperature ranges from 25 °C to 60 °C, 60 °C to 60 °C and 60 °C to 25 °C. Finally, repeated strain sweeps of Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 were carried out between 1000% and 1% with a frequency of 1 Hz to determine the damage-healing property of hydrogels.

The swelling equilibrium of Gel0-Gel5 was studied by the gravimetric method at different pH values. First, 40 mg of freeze-dried hydrogel samples were weighed (recorded as Wd) and immersed into 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4 or 5.0) to allow them to swell for 300 min at RT. Then, the swelled hydrogels were taken out, wiped with wet filter paper to remove the excess water on their surface, and weighed (recorded as Ws). Each sample was tested for three times. The water absorption ability of the samples was calculated by formula (1).
(1)
Water absorption ability
%
=
W s
−
W d
/
W d
× 100 %

Gel0, Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 were selected as representative sample to study the in vitro degradation ability at different pH value and temperature. Each wet hydrogel samples were weighed (recorded as W0), placed in a 2 mL centrifuge tube which containing 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4 or 5.0), and then incubated at 37 °C. At specified time intervals, the remaining weight of the samples was weighed (recorded as Wd). Each sample was tested for three times. The weight remaining ratio of Gel0, Gel1, and XL treated Gel1 were defined by formula (2).
(2)
Weight remaining ratio
%
=
W d
/
W 0
× 100 %

Gel1 with heart shape was cut into two parts evenly, and then reconnected and placed at RT without any external intervention. The reconnected hydrogel was slowly picked up with tweezers and photographed under VIS and UV254 nm light at specified time intervals. The change of the crack of the hydrogel was also observed by the fluorescence microscope (FM, IX71) at specified time intervals.

To analysis the in vitro antibacterial activity of the materials, E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were selected as model bacteria. The colonies of E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C with a shaking speed of 220 rpm for 24 h. Then, bacteria in the logarithmic growth phase were used for all antibacterial experiments.

100 μL of bacterial suspension (1 × 106 CFU/mL) was added to LB agar plate and spread evenly. And then, the sterilized oxford cups were put on the agar plate. GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL with a concentration of 4 mg/mL and Luria-Bertani (LB) medium was added into the oxford cups, respectively. After some samples were treated by XL (1 W/cm2), the agar plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Each sample was tested for three replicates, and the zones of the inhibition (ZOI) were photographed and estimated by subtracting the diameter of oxford cups from the diameter of the inhibition zone.

First, 200 μL of Gel0 and Gel1 were prepared at 48-well plates. Second, 300 μL of E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa suspension (1 × 106 CFU/mL) were added into the 48-well plates. After some Gel1 samples were irradiated by XL for 10 min (1 W/cm2), 500 μL of LB medium was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Finally, the upper bacterial suspensions were collected, and the optical density of the bacterial suspensions was measured by microplate reader (Spectra Max M5，Germany) at 600 nm. Each sample was tested for three replicates, and a control group without any treatment was also carried out. The bacterial viability was calculated by Eq. (3):
(3)
Bacterial viability
%
=
OD sample
−
OD blank
/
OD control
−
OD blank
× 100 %
where ODsample is the OD value of bacterial suspension treated with hydrogel samples, ODblank is the OD value of LB medium without bacteria, and ODcontrol is the OD value of bacterial suspension with LB medium.

To further investigate the bacterial activity, 100 μL of the above collected bacterial suspensions were spread onto the surfaces of LB agar plates, respectively. Then, the agar plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, and photographed.

100 μL of ONPG (10 mg/mL) and 200 μL of Gel0 and Gel1 were added into 1 mL of bacteria suspensions. After Gel1 + XL group was irradiated by XL for 10 min (1 W/cm2), the changes in the OD value at 420 nm were monitored by UV–vis spectrophotometer at different times. Each sample was tested in three replicates, and a control assay was also performed by the addition of ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of bacterial morphology by SEM
ce:section.ce:para: First, 300 μL of bacteria suspension was treated with 200 μL of Gel0 or Gel1 or Gel1 + XL (10 min, 1 W/cm2), and co-incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. The upper bacteria suspension were collected, centrifuged, and washed with PBS for three times. Then, the obtained bacteria were re-suspended and added a droplet onto the silicon wafer. The silicon wafer was fixed with 2.5% (v/v) glutaraldehyde overnight at 4 °C, carefully rinsed with PBS for three times, and dehydrated by sequential treatment with 30%, 50%, 75%, 90% and 100% ethanol (10 min for each gradient) and dried at RT. Finally, the obtained samples were observed by tungsten filament scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250) and the non-treated bacteria sample was used as control.

NIH3T3 cells were incubated in 48-well (5 × 103 cells/well) plate overnight. After replacing the culture medium, 2.5 mg/mL of the Gel0-Gel5 extracts were individual added. After further culturing for 48 h, NIH3T3 cells were harvested, washed with PBS and stained with 300 μL of AO/EB dye mixture (AO: 0.1 mg/mL, EB: 0.1 mg/mL) under dark conditions for 4 h. Then, the stained cells were washed and observed under the fluorescence microscope (IX71-F22PH; Olympus).

NIH3T3 cells were seeded (5 × 103 cell/well) in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2/95% O2. After replacing the culture medium, 0.125 mg, 0.25 mg, and 0.5 mg of the Gel0-Gel5 extracts were individual added. After further culturing for 24 h, NIH3T3 cells were harvested, washed with PBS, and 100 μL of 10% CCK-8 reagent was added into each well and re-incubated for 2 h. Finally, OD450 values were measured by microplate reader. Each sample was tested in three replicates, and the cell viability (%) was calculated by Eq. (4):
(4)
Cell viability
%
=
OD s
−
OD b
/
OD c
−
OD b
× 100 %
where ODs is the OD value of the hydrogel group, ODb is the OD value of the control group, and ODb is the OD value of the blank control group.

Fresh blood of Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (male, 200 g) was dispersed into 30 mL of 0.9% physiological saline containing heparin. The blood was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 3 min to obtain the erythrocytes. After washing three times with 0.9% physiological saline, erythrocytes were re-suspended in 0.9% physiological saline to form a 2% (wt) erythrocyte suspension. 50 μL of Gel0, Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 were added into 1 mL of erythrocyte suspension and incubated in a shaking table (60 rpm) at 37 °C for 3 h and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The absorbance of supernatant was analyzed at 540 nm by microplate reader. All samples were conducted three times and 0.9% physiological saline and 0.1% Triton X-100 were used as negative and positive control groups, respectively. The hemolysis ratio was calculated by formula (5):
(5)
Hemolysis ratio
%
=
OD s
−
OD n
/
OD o
−
OD n
× 100 %
where ODs is the absorbance value of hydrogel group, ODn is the absorbance value of 0.9% physiological saline and ODo is the absorbance value of 0.1%Triton x-100.

Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (male, 200 g) were purchased from the laboratory animal center of Fujian Medical University (certification number, SCXK 2016–0002). The care and use of laboratory animals were performed in compliance with the protocols approved by Fujian Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Fujian Medical University.

The liver of SD rats (200 g, male) was used as the hemostasis test model in this study. Firstly, SD rats were anesthetized with 2% sodium pentobarbital (2.5 mL/kg), then the ventral part of the rats was cut open with a scalpel and the liver SD rats was exposed. The surrounding body fluids were carefully wiped with cotton balls, and a pre-weighted filter paper was placed under the liver. After that, a wound about 1 cm in length and 0.5 cm in depth was cut at the largest lobe liver, and 200 μL Gel1 was injected to the wound site. After 10 min, the weight of the filter paper was weighed and the total amount of the blood loss was calculated. Each sample was conducted three times and a control group without treatment after cutting a wound on the liver was also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing of the infected wounds of diabetic rats
ce:section.ce:para: SD rats (200 g, male) were randomly selected to induce type I diabetes. Firstly, SD rats were fed for one week to adapt to the environment. After fasting for 12 h, the rats were intraperitoneally injected with fresh 1% streptozotocin (STZ) solution at a dose of 55 mg/kg [42]. After 48 h, blood was collected from the tail vein of the rats, and the blood glucose level of the rats under non-fasting condition was measured by a glucose meter. The blood glucose levels of the rats that were greater than 16.7 mmol/L were identified as type I diabetic rats. After a week of further observation, the blood glucose levels of the rats that were below than 16.7 mmol/L were directly eliminate.

The type I diabetic rats were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 2% sodium pentobarbital (2.5 mL/kg), and the dorsal area was depilated. The round full-thickness skin defect with a diameter of 1.0 cm was created on their upper back, respectively. The wounds of the rats were treated with 100 μL of mixture of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa (V:V = 1:1, 1 × 108 CFU/mL) suspensions for 2 d to form the infected diabetic wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repair assays on the infected wounds of diabetic rats
ce:section.ce:para:
The above infected diabetic wounds were treated with Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL (10 min, 1 W/cm2) (n = 6, marked as Gel0 (+), Gel1 (+) and Gel1 + XL (+) group, respectively). Meanwhile, the uninfected diabetic wounds treated with 0.9% saline and the infected diabetic wounds without any treatment were served as a negative control group (n = 6, marked as control (−)) and a positive control group (n = 6, marked as control (+)), respectively. All the treated rats were kept in separate cages and closely monitored during the experimental period. At 0 d, 3 d, 7 d and 14 d, wound areas were measured, and the wound contraction (%) was calculated using the following formula (6):
(6)
Wound contraction
%
=
Area 0
−
Area n
/
Area 0
× 100 %
where Area0 represents the wound area at 0 day, and Arean represents the wound area at n day.

The wound tissues were harvested at different time points, and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson staining was used to evaluate the histopathology of wound healing. Immunohistochemical analysis was performed by a Rat Collagen I (ColI) and CD31 Immunohistochemistry Kit to analysis collagen deposition and neovascularization.

All experiments data were analyzed and presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD), based on at least triplicated samples. Statistical differences between groups (****P < 0.0001，***P < 0.001，**P < 0.01 and *P < 0.05) were evaluated by using one-way ANOVA or student's t-test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by top-down approach using GO as raw material. Then, ε-PL was modified on the surface of GQDs by the amidation reaction of -COOH on GQDs and -NH2 on ε-PL. The structure diagram of GQDs-ε-PL was shown in Scheme 1 , and the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL were shown in Fig. 1a. The characteristic absorption peaks of GQDs were as follows: 1717 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration of -COOH), 1641 cm−1, 1424 cm−1 (frame stretching vibration of aromatic ring), 1096 cm−1 (CH in-plane bending vibration of aromatic ring), 877 cm−1 (CH out-of-plane bending vibration of aromatic ring). Compared to GQDs, the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs-ε-PL at 1717 cm−1 disappeared, and new characteristic absorption peaks at 1641 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration of amide bond) and 1513 cm−1 (NH in-plane bending vibration of amide bond) appeared, which indicating the successfully synthesis of GQDs-ε-PL. The micro-morphology of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL was also observed by TEM (Fig. 1b). GQDs were lamellar structure with a particle size about 22 nm. After grafting by ε-PL, the size of GQDs-ε-PL was increased to 53 nm with good dispersion, which was also proved the successfully synthesis of GQDs-ε-PL.

Since GQDs exists quantum confinement effect and surface/edge state, they are photoluminescent [43,44]. Hence, the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL were determined. As seen in Fig. 1c, both GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL exhibit a maximum emission wavelength at 490 nm when excited at 450 nm. But the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-ε-PL was weaker than GQDs, this is due to the grafting of ε-PL induced the aggregation of GQDs, and then the electronic structure changed, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. The UV–vis spectra of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL were also performed. It could be seen in Fig. 1d that due to the influence of the auxochrome groups (-NH2, -OH, and -COOH) on GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL, the maximum absorption wavelength which was belong to π-π* transition of the aromatic ring was red-shifted to about 285 nm. Moreover, due to the same concentration of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL contained different amounts of GQDs, resulting in the absorption peak intensity of GQDs-ε-PL was weaker than GQDs.

To demonstrate GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL were light responsive and had the photothermal effect, we recorded the temperature changes of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL aqueous solutions under xenon light (XL, wavelength range: 400–1100 nm) irradiation for 0–10 min. As seen in Fig. 1e, after being irradiated by XL for 10 min, the temperature of GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL rose to 54.1 °C and 47.1 °C. It will be beneficial to GQDs and GQDs-ε-PL play a photothermal sterilization effect under XL irradiation. Therefore, we further observed the inhibition zones of five groups (GQDs, GQDs+XL, LB + XL, GQDs-ε-PL and GQDs-ε-PL + XL) against E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa. By comparing the inhibition zones in Fig. 1f, we found that GQDs-ε-PL + XL group had the largest inhibition zones (1.9, 2.1 and 1.9 cm) against E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa. Furthermore, due to the grafting of ε-PL with antibacterial ability, GQDs-ε-PL also showed large inhibition zones (1.6, 1.6 and 1.5 cm) against E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa without XL irradiation. In contrast, the inhibition zones of GQDs were not outstanding. This is one reason we want to graft ε-PL on the surface of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structure characterization of GQDs-ε-PL/QCS/4 arm PEG-BA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Another purpose of synthesizing GQDs-ε-PL is to make the surface of the particles rich in amino groups, so that GQDs-ε-PL could be uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel network through dynamic imine bond crosslinking. Hence, GQDs-ε-PL and QCS, which are rich in amino groups on the molecules, and 4 arm PEG-BA, which is rich in aldehyde groups on the molecule were crosslinked in aqueous solution by dynamic imine bonds (-CH=N-, seeing Scheme 1), and then formed into semi-solid hydrogel quickly (seeing Fig. 2a). From Table 1, we can see that six kinds of hydrogels (Gel0-Gel5) were prepared by changing the feeding ratio of GQDs-ε-PL, QCS and 4 arm PEG-BA. The gelation time of Gel0-Gel5 at RT and 4 °C was also showed in Table 1. It could be seen that when the molar ratio of amino group to aldehyde group (n-NH2:n-CHO) was between 1.06:1 and 9.72:1, the gelation time at RT decreased with the increase of the amino content. This may be because the more the amino content, the easier it is to contact with the aldehyde group, which led to an increase in the crosslinking density and a shorter crosslinking time. However, when n-NH2:n-CHO>9.72:1, the gelation time began to increase, which was because the excessive amino groups increased the resistance between molecules, delayed the formation of dynamic imine bonds, and made the gel time longer. Similarly, the gelation time of the hydrogels at 4 °C also has a process of falling first and then rising. These results indicate that the gelation time could be regulated by changing of n-NH2:n-CHO or reaction temperature. In addition to in-situ gelation, the raw material solution can also be sprayed on the pigskin to form hydrogel. As seen in Fig. 2b, the spray gelation process was recorded in two ways (photographed under visible light and UV254 nm). After spraying the solutions on the pigskin, the solutions first formed a semi-gel state, followed by a hydrogel. And then stand up the pigskin, the hydrogel would not fall off. Since the hydrogel networks contained fluorescent GQDs, photos taken under UV254 nm light could see the gelation process more clearly. At the same time, the GQDs containing hydrogel could also form into different shapes, and showed a blue color under UV254 nm light (Fig. 2c). After gelation, the “soft” hydrogel could still be injected out of the syringe (Fig. 2c). Therefore, the injectability and different gelation method of the hydrogel were conducive to the closure of wounds of different shapes to closely fit varying degrees of wounds.

The chemical structure of Gel1 was characterized by FT-IR with the solid mixture of GQDs-ε-PL, QCS and 4 arm PEG-BA as comparison. As shown in Fig. 2d, the characteristic peaks of the solid mixture were 3418 cm−1 (NH and OH stretching vibration on GQDs-ε-PL and QCS), 2878 cm−1 (CH stretching vibration), 1703 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration of benzaldehyde on 4 arm PEG-BA), 1649 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration of amide bonds on 4 arm PEG-BA) and 1113 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration on QCS and 4 arm PEG-BA). Comparing to the spectrum of the solid mixture, the characteristic peaks of Gel1 changed. First, the CO stretching vibration of benzaldehyde at 1703 cm−1 disappeared, and then the peak at 1660 cm−1 which was assigned to CN stretching vibration of imine bonds and CO stretching vibration of amide bonds enhanced, indicating the formation of lots of imine bonds and the successful preparation of the hydrogel.

The morphological structure of Gel0-Gel5 was characterized by SEM and CLSM. As seen in Fig. 3 , all the hydrogels displayed porous network structure. Since the surface of GQDs-ε-PL is rich in amino groups, the crosslinking points for hydrogel formation are increased, resulting in the thickness of the pore wall becoming thicker with the increase of GQDs content. From the CLSM images, we could see that the presence of green fluorescent GQDs made the network structure of the hydrogels more clearly visible. The green fluorescent GQDs were uniformly distributed on the pore wall, and as the content of GQDs increased, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel enhanced, and the thickness of the pore wall increased, which was basically consistent with the SEM results. The uniform distributing of GQDs in the network of the hydrogels will beneficial to exert uniform photothermal effect on the wound to fight infections. Moreover, since the crosslinking points of the hydrogel were relatively uniform, the appearance of hydrogels was almost transparent, which will be conducive to observe the wound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Xenon light responsive photothermal performance of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Since GQDs-ε-PL was distributed uniformly in the network of the hydrogels, the photothermal capacities of the hydrogels were also investigated. As seen in Fig. 4a, when irradiated by XL for 15 min, except for Gel0 that did not contain GQDs, the temperature of all other hydrogels has reached more than 50 °C. As the content of GQDs in the hydrogel network increased, the temperature raised higher. The results in Fig. 4b were consistent to Fig. 4a, indicating the hydrogels had good photothermal performance. Besides, the repeatable heating capacity was also studied. After subjecting to four cycles of repeated on/off XL irradiation, the temperature of Gel1 still could rise to above 50 °C (Fig. 4c), indicating the photothermal conversion ability of the hydrogels could be repeatedly excited, which is better than some organic fluorescent dyes. Thus, we can repeatedly stimulate the photothermal conversion ability of the hydrogel to fight infection during wound treatment.

After conducting the photothermal performance test, we found that the state of the XL treated hydrogels changed after being placed for 24 h. To confirm this, we used a rheometer to study the mechanical properties of Gel0-Gel5 and XL treated Gel1. As shown in Fig. 4d, the storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) of Gel0-Gel5 and XL treated Gel1 were recorded in the range of angular frequency from 1 to 10 rad s−1. The G′ of all the hydrogels was greater than G″ during the angular frequency sweep and G′ of all the hydrogels hardly changed with the angular frequency, indicating Gel0-Gel5 and XL treated Gel1 were in the state of hydrogel network, and the network structures were stable. Moreover, the G′ value of the hydrogels was Gel1 > Gel2 > Gel3 > Gel4 ≈ Gel5 > Gel0. The mechanical properties of Gel1 were the best, and that of Gel0 was the worst. This is because the addition of an appropriate amount of GQDs-ε-PL enhanced the dynamic imine bond crosslinking density and improved the mechanical properties of Gel1. However, as the amount of GQDs-ε-PL increased, the excessive amino groups increased the resistance between molecules, resulting in the G′ value of Gel2-Gel5 reduced. Most interestingly, after treating by XL, the G′ value of XL treated Gel1 became higher than Gel1, indicating the XL irradiation will not affect the properties of the hydrogel, but make the mechanical properties of the hydrogels better. This is conducive to better closure of the wound.

Given the above interesting results and referring to previous literature [45,46], we further observed the effect of temperature on the mechanical properties of the hydrogels by simulating the heating-constant temperature-cooling-constant temperature process of the hydrogels under XL irradiation. As seen in Fig. 5b, in the heating range of 25–60 °C, the G′ of the hydrogels was always greater than G″, but the G′ value decreased as the temperature rose. This is because the thermal motion of molecules increased with the increase of the temperature, which increased the distance between molecules and decreased the degree of crosslinking, resulting in a tendency for the hydrogels to become sols. In the constant temperature range of 60–60 °C, the G′ and G″ values of all the hydrogels increased. This is because the macromolecular chains were in curled states at high temperature, which made the inter-molecular gaps smaller and the gel shrinks. When the temperature stabilized at 60 °C, the molecular thermal motion gradually became orderly, and the crosslinking sites started to recover, resulting in the rapid rise of the G′ and G″ values. In the cooling range of 60–25 °C, the G′ value of the hydrogels firstly increased, which is because the thermal movement speed gradually slowed down as the temperature decreased, and most of the crosslinking sites were restored; and then the G′ value decreased, which is due to the macromolecular chain gradually stretched from the bent state. When the temperature gradually decreased to RT, the inter-molecular gaps became larger, leading to a certain break of the crosslinking sites. In the constant temperature range of 25–25 °C, the thermal motion of molecules weakened, and the influence of temperature on G′ and G″ values gradually decreased and tended to stabilize. In general, after four stages of temperature changing, the G′ value of the final hydrogels raised (Gel5, Gel1, and Gel3 raised more obviously), which is because the interactions between the molecules were more uniform and the crosslinking was more dense after the molecular thermal movement, resulting in promotion of the mechanical properties of the hydrogels. The results are consistent to Fig. 4d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent swelling ability and degradability of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Literatures have reported that dynamic imine bonds are unstable and easily undergo protonation at acidic environment [35,36]. Hence, the swelling ability and degradability of the dynamic imine bonds crosslinked hydrogels at pH =7.4 and 5.0 was investigated. It could be seen in Fig. 4f that at the same pH, Gel0 had the best water absorption ability (3330% at pH = 7.4, 1633% at pH = 5.0), while the water absorption ability of Gel1-Gel5 became weaker as the content of GQDs-ε-PL increased. This may be because the addition of GQDs-ε-PL increased the crosslinking density of the hydrogels, resulting in a decrease in the water storage capacity of the hydrogels. Comparing the swelling ability of the same hydrogel at different pH, we found that at acidic environment (pH = 5.0), the swelling ability of the hydrogels became weaker. This is because the dynamic imine bonds are unstable and easily undergo protonation under acidic conditions, leading to partial degradation of the hydrogels, and then the swelling ratio of the hydrogels became smaller.

As seen in Fig. 4g, taking Gel0, Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 as representatives, the degradation behavior of the wet hydrogels under pH = 7.4 and 5.0 at 37 °C was observed. All the hydrogels experienced a stage of swelling and then degradation at pH = 7.4. However, due to the poor mechanical properties of wet Gel0, it degraded before reaching the maximum swelling degree and completely degraded at 36 h. On the contrary, for Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 with better mechanical properties, they could absorb water and swelled to the greatest extent before degradation, and the degradation has not been completed after 48 h. Moreover, we could see that the degradation rate of XL treated Gel1 was the slowest, there was still 80% remaining at 48 h, further showing that XL irradiation could improve the performance of the hydrogels. At pH = 5.0, the degradation rate of all the hydrogels became faster, Gel0, Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 were completely degraded at 12, 24 and 36 h, respectively, showing a pH-dependent degradation.

As we known, when a bacterial infection occurs in the wounds, the surface of the wounds will be insufficiently supplied with blood due to local tissue vasoconstriction, resulting in hypoxia and increased glycolysis, which will increase lactic acid and decrease the pH of the wound surface [37,38]. The bacteria triggered acidic wound surface will accelerate the degradation of the hydrogels (the dynamic imine bonds broke) to release QCS and GQDs-ε-PL for chemical and photothermal synergistic sterilization to promote wound healing (Mechanism diagram seeing Scheme 1). Moreover, the degradation of the hydrogel could also prevent the patient from tearing pain when changing the dressing.

During the use of the dressings, they will inevitably be damaged by external forces, resulting in their inability to effectively seal the wound. Literatures have reported that the hydrogels crosslinked by dynamic covalent bonds can self-heal after being damaged [47,48], which is conducive to the hydrogels to seal the wound and provide a good healing environment for the wound. Therefore, we investigated the self-healing ability of the hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 5a, the damage and healing process of Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 were analyzed using a rheometer by alternating strain sweep from 1% to 1000%. Both Gel1 and XL treated Gel1 was damaged and changed into sol states (G′ < G″) under 500% strain in 2 min. While the strain decreased to 1%, the sol states turned back into gel states (G′ > G″), immediately. Go through three alternating strain sweeps from 1% to 1000%, the sol to gel and sol to gel transformation process could still be carried out, indicating the hydrogels had the potential to recover their structures after being destroyed by high strain and displayed a good self-healing ability. It is worth mentioning that although XL treated Gel1 was irradiated by XL, it did not affect its performance, which maintained the same self-healing ability as Gel1.

In order to explore the self-healing ability of the hydrogels in practical applications, the self-healing process of Gel1 under the macroscopic (visible and UV254 nm light) and microscopic (fluorescence microscope) angles was further observed. As seen in Fig. 5b, the heart-shaped hydrogel was cut in half and rejoined, the cracks between the two halves gradually decreased with time. After 20 min, the rejoined hydrogels could be picked up with tweezers without external intervention, and it was found from fluorescence microscope observation that the cracks finally disappeared at 68 min. The possible self-healing mechanism was shown in Fig. 5c, when the hydrogel was damaged by external force, the dynamic imine bonds were broke. But due to the dynamic characteristics of the dynamic imine bond, the broken imine bond could be restored after a certain period of time, and then resulting in the self-healing of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The ideal wound dressings should have excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial ability in addition to good physical and chemical properties. In the above, it has been proved that the hydrogels degraded quicker in acidic infected wounds, so that QCS and GQDs-ε-PL with antibacterial properties will expose more. Hence, it is necessary to evaluate the single antibacterial effect and synergistic antibacterial effect of the hydrogels. As seen in Fig. 6a, the bacterial viability of Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL treated E. coli was 66.5%, 10.2% and 3.4%; the bacterial viability of Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL treated S. aureus was 42.0%, 11.8% and 2.9%; the bacterial viability of Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL treated P. aeruginosa was 80.8%, 19.0% and 2.0%, respectively. Since Gel0 only contained the antibacterial QCS and did not contain GQDs-ε-PL, its antibacterial activity is not as good as Gel1. Moreover, Gel1 exhibited the best antibacterial ability after XL irradiation for 10 min, indicating that Gel1 could exert chemical and photothermal synergistic effects to achieve the best antibacterial effect. To further investigate the antibacterial activity of the hydrogels, the above Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL treated bacterial suspensions were spread onto the surfaces of LB agar plates and incubated for another 12 h. The results were shown in Fig. 6b, no colonies appeared on the agar plates of Gel1 + XL treated groups; and only a few colonies appeared on the agar plates of Gel1 treated groups. Although a large number of colonies appeared on the agar plates of Gel0 groups and the control groups, the number of colonies Gel0 groups was less than that in the control group. The results are similar to the results in Fig. 8a, indicating the good antibacterial activity of the hydrogels.
The antibacterial mechanism of the hydrogels was studied by inner membrane permeabilization assay and SEM method.

Studies have shown that β-galactosidase will be released after the bacterial inner membrane is ruptured, and ONPG can react with β-galactosidase in the plasma membrane of the cell to produce ONP [49,50]. Therefore, the detection of the OD value of ONP at 420 nm by a UV spectrophotometer can reflect the rupture of the bacterial inner membrane. As seen in Fig. 7a, all the Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL groups caused damage to the inner membrane of E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa. The groups of Gel1 + XL treated inner membranes had the most serious damage, followed by Gel1 Groups and Gel0 groups, which is consistent with the above antibacterial activity results. To observe the rupture of the bacterial membrane more intuitively, Gel1 + XL treated bacteria were observed by SEM with non-treated bacteria as control. As shown in Fig. 7b, the non-treated E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa exhibited a bright and smooth surface which indicating the bacteria were protected by intact cytoplasmic membrane. However, the Gel1 + XL treated E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa displayed a severe membrane disruption, shrink and cytoplasm leakage, further demonstrating the chemical and photothermal synergistic antibacterial effects of the hydrogels. Consequently, the antibacterial mechanism of the hydrogels may be that showed in Fig. 7c. First, Gel1 treated bacteria could quickly heat up after being irradiated with XL for 10 min. Then, the high temperature destroyed the bacterial cell membrane directly and changed the permeability of the bacterial cell membrane. Thus, the antibacterial substances GQDs-ε-PL and QCS were easier to interact with the bacteria to destroy the bacterial cell membrane, and resulting in the leaking of cytoplasm and dead of the bacteria. The synergistic effects by chemotherapy and PTT together led to the rupture of the bacterial membrane and the inactivation of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to hemostasis and antibacterial capabilities, the dressings should also possess good compatibility. Hence, the cytocompatibility and the hemocompatibility of the hydrogels were evaluated by AO/EB staining, CCK-8 assays, and hemolysis test. As shown in Fig. 8a, NIH3T3 cells were green and alive after co-incubating with extracts of Gel0-Gel5 for 48 h, and there were almost no red and dead cells, indicating the hydrogels had no cytotoxicity to NIH3T3 cells. Similarly, the cell viabilities in Fig. 8b were more than 80% when NIH3T3 cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of Gel0-Gel5 extracts for 24 h, also indicating the hydrogels had good cytocompatibility to NIH3T3 cells. Fig. 8c showed hemolysis photos and hemolysis ratios of Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL with Triton X-100 as control. It could be seen that except for the bright red supernatant of Triton X-100 group, the other groups were all light yellow supernatants, ant the hemolysis ratios of Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL groups were 2.7%, 2.4% and 3.6%, respectively. The data were less than 5%, illustrating Gel0, Gel1 and Gel1 + XL groups had no obvious toxicity and possessed good hemocompatibility. Moreover, the XL irradiation almost had no effect on the hemocompatibility of hydrogels. These positive results will allow the hydrogels to be used as potential wound dressings.

When injured, bleeding is ineluctable. A dressing with a hemostatic effect will promote cell proliferation and remodeling of the cell matrix in the wound healing stage, thereby accelerating the healing process of the wound [51]. Hence, a bleeding liver model was created and covered with hydrogel at the wound to evaluate the hemostatic ability of the hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 8d, the blood loss from the rat's liver in Gel1 group was only about 58 mg, but it was much higher (241.0 mg) in the control group. The excellent hemostatic ability of the hydrogel is mainly attributed from its adhesion property and water absorption capability, which can adhere to the wound sites, absorb blood exudates, and provide a stable physical barrier to prevent bleeding (Fig. 8e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The repairing effect of the hydrogels on the infected diabetic wounds
ce:section.ce:para: The above results have shown that the hydrogels had pH-dependent swelling ability and degradability, self-healing ability, antibacterial activity, hemostatic performance and in vitro biocompatibility, which are the characteristics that an ideal wound dressing should possess. Thus, the hydrogels are expected to have the ability to promote the healing of wounds (especially diabetic wounds). As illustrated in Scheme 1, when the hydrogel are administrated on the infected wound of diabetic rat and irradiated by XL for 10 min, the infected wound can first be heated up, and then the acidic infected wound environment and the high temperature will trigger the degradation of hydrogel to release antibacterial substances GQDs-ε-PL and QCS. Hence, the bacteria in the wound are killed by synergistic effects of PTT and chemotherapy. Afterwards, the remaining hydrogel will self-heal by re-crosslinking of dynamic imine bonds to provide a close environment for wound.

To confirm the above supposes, the healing process of infected wounds of diabetic rats was investigated. As shown in Fig. 9a and b, at 0 d, except for the control (−) group, all the other four groups were severely infected diabetic wounds; At 3 d, the contraction rates of control (+), Gel0 (+), Gel1 (+) and Gel1 + XL (+) and control (−) groups were 0.0%, 6.7%, 10.0%, 18.3% and 21.7%, respectively. The wounds in control (+) group did not contract and was still severely infected, while those of Gel0 (+), Gel1 (+) and Gel1 + XL (+) and control (−) groups began to scab and contract. The wounds in the Gel1 + XL (+) group were not burnt to black due to the XL radiation heating, indicating that XL radiation is relatively mild, unlike near-infrared light radiation that will scorch the skin; At 7 d, the infection in control (+) group was relieved due to autoimmunity of the rats, and the wound began to scab. The contraction rates of Gel1 (+), Gel1 + XL (+) and control (−) groups increased significantly, and the healing speed of Gel1 + XL (+) and control (−) group was equivalent, which proved that the synergistic antibacterial effect of PTT and chemotherapy promoted the rapid healing of wounds and achieved the effect of simultaneous healing with uninfected wounds; At 14 d, the new full-thickness skin had almost filled the entire wound area in Gel1 + XL (+) and control (−) groups, while control (+), Gel0 (+) and Gel1 (+) group had not healed completely. The results showed that the GQDs based antibacterial hydrogels could effectively promote the healing of infected wounds in diabetic rats by synergistic effect of PTT and chemotherapy.

The wound tissue is rich in fibroblasts and various growth factors, which can be used as an indicator of wound healing [52]. It is necessary to evaluate the specific therapeutic effect of the hydrogel on the infected wounds of diabetic rats through histopathological analysis, such as H&E staining, Masson staining and immunohistochemical analysis. From the H&E staining results shown in Fig. 9c and d, we could see that the inflammation of each wound tissue was alleviated over time due to the rat's own immune function and the antibacterial effect of the hydrogels. But it is worth mentioning that the number of inflammatory cells in Gel1 + XL (+) group was significantly less than that in the control (+) group, which proved that the hydrogel could reduce the wound infection of diabetic rats through the combination of photothermal and chemical treatment, and could also closely fit the wound, protect the wound from external bacterial. The results of Masson staining, Col-I and CD31 protein expression employed to observe the collagen deposition in the skin cambium, and the formation of new blood vessels during wound healing were showed in Fig. 9e, the collagen fibers dyed blue by Masson staining were the most abundant in the wound tissue of the Gel1 + XL (+) group, and the expression of Col-I and CD31 proteins was also the most obvious in the wound tissue of Gel1 + XL (+) group, indicating the synergistic PTT and chemotherapy of hydrogels could effectively kill the bacteria, promote fibroblast cell migration and proliferation, and enhanced platelet endothelial cell adhesion by upregulating the expression of CD31 protein, which ultimately accelerated wound tissue healing.

In summary, this work used QCS, 4 arm PEG-BA and GQDs-ε-PL as materials to prepare antibacterial hydrogels through dynamic imine bond cross-linking for effective treatment of DU. The uniform distribution of GQDs-ε-PL in the hydrogel made mechanical property of the hydrogels better. The in situ, sprayable and injectable gelation methods enabled the hydrogels to seal and adhere wounds of different shapes. The hydrogels were proved to have pH sensitivity, xenon light (XL) induced photothermal effect, which could degrade at the bacterial triggered acidic environment to release more antibacterial substances QCS and GQDs-ε-PL to reduce inflammatory of infected diabetic wound by synergistic chemo-photothermal antibacterial effect without burning the wound. The hydrogels could also self-heal to provide a close environment for wound, further promoting the deposition of collagen and the formation of new blood vessels, and finally facilitating the healing of the infected diabetic wound. The hydrogels have the potential to provide an “intelligent” strategy for the anti-infective and multifunctional treatment of DU.
